answer:
Comparing MongoDB Aggregation Approaches

1. Definition & Context

MongoDB Aggregation is a pipeline-based data processing framework that transforms and computes results from collections. Two approaches: Aggregation Pipeline (server-side, optimized stages like $match, $group, $lookup) and Application-Level Aggregation (fetch documents and process in Node.js with Array methods). The pipeline approach is almost always preferred for production because it runs on the database server, avoiding network overhead and leveraging indexes.

Key stages: $match (filter), $group (aggregate), $project (reshape), $lookup (join), $unwind (flatten arrays), $facet (parallel pipelines), $sort/$limit/$skip (pagination).

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the pipeline chart showed leads grouped by status with count and total deal value. Initially, the code fetched all 500+ leads and grouped them in JavaScript: leads.reduce((acc, lead) => ...). This transferred 500 documents (~200KB) over the network for a result that was 6 rows. Manoj refactored to a MongoDB aggregation pipeline that returned only the 6 grouped rows directly from the server.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// Approach A: MongoDB Aggregation Pipeline (CRM Dashboard)
const pipelineStats = await Lead.aggregate([
  { $match: { createdAt: { $gte: startOfMonth } } },  // Uses index
  { $group: {
    _id: '$status',
    count: { $sum: 1 },
    totalValue: { $sum: '$dealValue' },
    avgValue: { $avg: '$dealValue' }
  }},
  { $sort: { count: -1 } },
  { $project: {
    status: '$_id',
    count: 1,
    totalValue: { $round: ['$totalValue', 2] },
    avgValue: { $round: ['$avgValue', 2] },
    _id: 0
  }}
]);
// Returns: [{ status: 'New', count: 150, totalValue: 45000, avgValue: 300 }, ...]
// Network: ~500 bytes instead of 200KB

// Approach B: App-level processing (what Manoj replaced)
const leads = await Lead.find({ createdAt: { $gte: startOfMonth } });
const stats = Object.entries(
  leads.reduce((acc, lead) => {
    if (!acc[lead.status]) acc[lead.status] = { count: 0, totalValue: 0 };
    acc[lead.status].count++;
    acc[lead.status].totalValue += lead.dealValue;
    return acc;
  }, {})
).map(([status, data]) => ({
  status,
  count: data.count,
  totalValue: Math.round(data.totalValue * 100) / 100,
  avgValue: Math.round((data.totalValue / data.count) * 100) / 100
}));
// Fetches 500+ documents just to compute 6 rows

5. Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

Symptom: The pipeline chart took 3 seconds to load on Monday mornings when many leads were created over the weekend. CPU usage on the Node.js server spiked to 80%.

Investigation: Manoj profiled the dashboard API and found the Lead.find() call fetched 800 documents. The JavaScript reduce() processing took 200ms, but the MongoDB query + network transfer took 2.5 seconds.

Root Cause: Fetching all documents to compute aggregate statistics was an O(n) network operation. As data grew, it got linearly slower.

Fix: Switched to aggregation pipeline. Added a compound index on { createdAt: 1, status: 1, dealValue: 1 } (covered index — the query never touches documents). Response time dropped from 3 seconds to 50ms. CPU usage dropped from 80% to 5% because aggregation ran on MongoDB server, not Node.js.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
No index for $match stage (collection scan)Place $match first and ensure matching index exists
$lookup on large collections (slow join)Add index on foreign field; consider denormalization
Pipeline returns huge result setAdd $limit or use $facet for pagination
Hard to debug intermediate stagesUse $out to temp collection or .explain() for plan

7. Validation Strategy

  • Manoj's choice: Aggregation pipeline for all analytics and reporting queries
  • Performance test: Benchmark with 10K documents — pipeline should be <100ms
  • Explain plan: Run .explain('executionStats') to verify index usage
  • Result test: Compare pipeline output with app-level computation for same dataset

Designing MongoDB Aggregation for Production

1. Definition & Context

Designing production-grade MongoDB aggregation involves building efficient pipelines that leverage indexes, handle large datasets, and produce results for dashboards, reports, and analytics. Key design decisions: pipeline ordering (filter early with $match), index coverage (compound indexes for covered queries), result shaping ($project, $addFields), and pagination ($facet with parallel count + data pipelines).

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the reports page needed: (1) leads grouped by source with conversion rates, (2) monthly revenue trend, and (3) team performance rankings — all from the same leads collection. Manoj used $facet to run all three aggregations in a single database round-trip, reducing API response time from 3 separate queries (~450ms total) to 1 faceted query (~120ms).

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// MongoDB $facet for CRM Dashboard reports
const reportData = await Lead.aggregate([
  // Common filter applied to all facets
  { $match: {
    createdAt: { $gte: sixMonthsAgo },
    isDeleted: { $ne: true }
  }},
  { $facet: {
    // Facet 1: Leads by source with conversion rate
    bySource: [
      { $group: {
        _id: '$source',
        total: { $sum: 1 },
        won: { $sum: { $cond: [{ $eq: ['$status', 'won'] }, 1, 0] } },
        revenue: { $sum: { $cond: [{ $eq: ['$status', 'won'] }, '$dealValue', 0] } }
      }},
      { $addFields: {
        conversionRate: {
          $round: [{ $multiply: [{ $divide: ['$won', '$total'] }, 100] }, 1]
        }
      }},
      { $sort: { revenue: -1 } }
    ],
    // Facet 2: Monthly revenue trend
    monthlyRevenue: [
      { $match: { status: 'won' } },
      { $group: {
        _id: { $dateToString: { format: '%Y-%m', date: '$closedAt' } },
        revenue: { $sum: '$dealValue' },
        deals: { $sum: 1 }
      }},
      { $sort: { '_id': 1 } }
    ],
    // Facet 3: Team performance ranking
    teamRanking: [
      { $group: {
        _id: '$assignedTo',
        totalLeads: { $sum: 1 },
        wonDeals: { $sum: { $cond: [{ $eq: ['$status', 'won'] }, 1, 0] } },
        totalRevenue: { $sum: { $cond: [{ $eq: ['$status', 'won'] }, '$dealValue', 0] } }
      }},
      { $lookup: {
        from: 'users',
        localField: '_id',
        foreignField: '_id',
        as: 'user'
      }},
      { $unwind: '$user' },
      { $project: { name: '$user.name', totalLeads: 1, wonDeals: 1, totalRevenue: 1 } },
      { $sort: { totalRevenue: -1 } },
      { $limit: 10 }
    ]
  }}
]);

5. Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

Symptom: The $facet pipeline worked fast in development (50 leads) but timed out in staging with 5000 leads. The teamRanking facet took 8 seconds.

Investigation: Manoj ran .explain('executionStats') and found the $lookup to the users collection was performing a full collection scan for each of the 20 unique assignees — no index on users._id for the local field match.

Root Cause: The $lookup foreign field was _id (automatically indexed), but the localField was assignedTo which stored user IDs as strings while users._id was ObjectId. Type mismatch caused the lookup to fail silently and return empty arrays for every user.

Fix: Manoj ensured assignedTo was stored as ObjectId (not string) in the leads collection. He added a migration script to convert existing string IDs to ObjectIds. After the fix, the $lookup used the _id index correctly and the entire pipeline completed in 80ms.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
$lookup type mismatch (string vs ObjectId)Ensure consistent types; use $toObjectId in pipeline if needed
$facet doesn't use indexes per facetPlace $match before $facet to filter early
Memory limit (100MB per stage)Use allowDiskUse: true for large aggregations
$unwind on empty arrays removes documentsUse { $unwind: { preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true } }

7. Validation Strategy

  • Explain plan: Verify all $match stages use indexes (IXSCAN not COLLSCAN)
  • Correctness test: Compare aggregation output with manual calculation on test data
  • Scale test: Run with 50K documents — must complete in <500ms
  • Type test: Verify $lookup returns non-empty arrays (catches type mismatches)

Designing Mongoose Modeling for Production

1. Definition & Context

Mongoose modeling involves designing MongoDB schemas with the right balance of embedding vs referencing, choosing appropriate schema types, configuring indexes, and defining middleware hooks. Key decisions: embed vs reference (embed for data accessed together, reference for large/shared data), schema design patterns (polymorphic, bucket, computed), and virtuals/methods/statics for domain logic.

Production concerns: TTL indexes for auto-expiry, compound indexes for query patterns, schema validation with required/enum/validate, and population strategies for related data.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contact schema needed to support: multiple phone numbers (embedded array), an avatar image URL from Cloudinary, tags for categorization, and a link to the user who created it (reference). Manoj chose to embed phone numbers (always accessed with the contact, max 5 per contact) and reference the user (shared across many contacts, would cause data bloat if embedded). He added a compound index on { userId: 1, name: 1 } for the user's contact search and a text index on { name: 'text', email: 'text', tags: 'text' } for full-text search.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Mongoose schema design (ContactsHub)
import mongoose, { Schema, Document } from 'mongoose';

interface IContact extends Document {
  name: string;
  email?: string;
  phones: { type: string; number: string }[];
  avatar?: string;
  tags: string[];
  userId: mongoose.Types.ObjectId;
  createdAt: Date;
  updatedAt: Date;
}

const contactSchema = new Schema({
  name: {
    type: String,
    required: [true, 'Name is required'],
    trim: true,
    maxlength: [100, 'Name cannot exceed 100 characters']
  },
  email: {
    type: String,
    lowercase: true,
    trim: true,
    sparse: true,  // Allows multiple nulls with unique index
    validate: {
      validator: (v: string) => !v || /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(v),
      message: 'Invalid email format'
    }
  },
  phones: [{
    type: { type: String, enum: ['mobile', 'work', 'home'], default: 'mobile' },
    number: { type: String, required: true }
  }],
  avatar: String,
  tags: [{ type: String, lowercase: true, trim: true }],
  userId: { type: Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'User', required: true, index: true }
}, {
  timestamps: true,
  toJSON: { virtuals: true }
});

// Compound index for user's contact search
contactSchema.index({ userId: 1, name: 1 });
// Text index for full-text search
contactSchema.index({ name: 'text', email: 'text', tags: 'text' });
// TTL index for soft-deleted contacts (auto-purge after 30 days)
contactSchema.index({ deletedAt: 1 }, { expireAfterSeconds: 2592000, sparse: true });

// Pre-save middleware: normalize phone numbers
contactSchema.pre('save', function(next) {
  this.phones = this.phones.map(p => ({
    ...p,
    number: p.number.replace(/[^\d+]/g, '')
  }));
  next();
});

// Virtual: display name with primary phone
contactSchema.virtual('summary').get(function() {
  return `${this.name} (${this.phones[0]?.number || 'no phone'})`;
});

export const Contact = mongoose.model('Contact', contactSchema);

5. Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Symptom: The contact search endpoint was fast for the first 100 contacts but degraded to 2 seconds with 5000 contacts. Searching by name within a user's contacts was especially slow.

Investigation: Manoj ran db.contacts.find({ userId: ObjectId('...'), name: /john/i }).explain('executionStats') and saw COLLSCAN — full collection scan examining all 5000 documents.

Root Cause: The compound index was { userId: 1, name: 1 }, but the query used a regex for name search (/john/i). MongoDB can use the index for the userId prefix but cannot efficiently use the name part of the compound index with a case-insensitive regex.

Fix: Manoj added a text index on name and switched the search query from regex to $text search: { userId: objectId, $text: { $search: 'john' } }. For the autocomplete use case, he added a nameNormalized field (lowercase, trimmed) with an index and used a prefix regex: { nameNormalized: /^john/ } which can use the index efficiently.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Case-insensitive regex can't use indexUse text index or normalized lowercase field with prefix regex
Embedding large arrays (>100 items)Reference instead; or use bucket pattern
Missing sparse on optional unique fieldAdd sparse: true to allow multiple nulls
populate() N+1 queriesUse $lookup in aggregation for batch joins

7. Validation Strategy

  • Schema validation test: Create contact with missing required fields — assert validation error
  • Index test: Run explain() on common queries — verify IXSCAN not COLLSCAN
  • Scale test: Insert 10K contacts, run search — must complete in <100ms
  • Migration test: Add new field with default — verify backward compatibility

Debugging Mongoose Modeling Issues

1. Definition & Context

Mongoose modeling bugs include schema mismatches (wrong types, missing fields), middleware side effects, population failures, and version conflicts. These bugs are often silent — Mongoose casts types, ignores unknown fields, and returns null instead of throwing errors, making issues hard to detect until production.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, users reported that updating a contact's tags via the API worked fine, but the contact reverted to old tags after any subsequent save. The issue only happened when another field was updated after the tag update.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// BUG: document.save() overwrites concurrent changes
app.patch('/api/contacts/:id', async (req, res) => {
  // BAD: Read-modify-write pattern
  const contact = await Contact.findById(req.params.id);
  contact.phone = req.body.phone;  // Only updating phone
  await contact.save();  // But this saves ALL fields, including stale tags!
});

// FIX 1: Use updateOne with $set (partial update)
app.patch('/api/contacts/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const result = await Contact.findOneAndUpdate(
    { _id: req.params.id },
    { $set: req.body },  // Only updates provided fields
    { new: true, runValidators: true }
  );
  res.json(result);
});

// FIX 2: If using save(), mark only modified paths
app.patch('/api/contacts/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const contact = await Contact.findById(req.params.id);
  Object.keys(req.body).forEach(key => {
    contact[key] = req.body[key];
    contact.markModified(key);  // Only these fields will be sent
  });
  await contact.save();  // Mongoose only sends modified paths
});

// FIX 3: Optimistic concurrency with version check
app.patch('/api/contacts/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const result = await Contact.findOneAndUpdate(
    { _id: req.params.id, __v: req.body.__v },
    { $set: req.body, $inc: { __v: 1 } },
    { new: true, runValidators: true }
  );
  if (!result) {
    return res.status(409).json({ error: 'Contact modified by another user' });
  }
  res.json(result);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Symptom: After adding a new notes field to the Contact schema, existing contacts returned notes: undefined. But new contacts had the default value of ''. Re-saving an old contact also didn't add the notes field to the MongoDB document.

Investigation: Manoj used mongosh to inspect raw documents: db.contacts.findOne({ _id: ObjectId('...') }). The old documents didn't have a notes field at all. Mongoose returned undefined for missing fields but didn't write the default value because save() only sends modified fields.

Root Cause: Mongoose's default only applies when creating new documents. Existing documents without the field return undefined from Mongoose (the default isn't retroactively applied). The field was undefined in the POJO representation but missing from MongoDB.

Fix: Manoj ran a migration script: db.contacts.updateMany({ notes: { $exists: false } }, { $set: { notes: '' } }). He also added a Mongoose getter that returned the default for missing values: get: v => v ?? ''. For future schema changes, he created a migration pattern using a schemaVersion field.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
save() overwrites concurrent changesUse findOneAndUpdate with $set for partial updates
Schema defaults not applied to existing docsRun migration to backfill; or use getter for fallback
Missing runValidators on updateAlways pass { runValidators: true } to update operations
populate() returns null for deleted referencesHandle null in response; or use $lookup with preserveNullAndEmptyArrays

7. Validation Strategy

  • Concurrency test: Two simultaneous updates to different fields — verify both preserved
  • Migration test: Add new field, query old document — verify default value returned
  • Validation test: Update with invalid data via findOneAndUpdate — assert validation error
  • Schema version test: Load document with old schema version — verify auto-migration works

Designing Data Partitioning

1. Definition & Context

Data partitioning (sharding) distributes data across multiple database nodes to handle scale beyond a single machine's capacity. Two strategies: horizontal partitioning (rows distributed across nodes by a shard key — e.g., by tenant, region, or hash) and vertical partitioning (columns split across tables — e.g., frequently accessed fields separate from large blobs). For mid-size products, the practical question is when to shard (often: don't until needed) and how to design the shard key for even distribution.

MongoDB's sharding uses a shard key to distribute documents across shards. Key choices: hashed sharding (even distribution, no range queries) vs ranged sharding (supports range queries, risk of hot spots).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, while the system didn't need sharding at 200 employees, he designed the PostgreSQL schema with partitioning in mind. The leave_audit_logs table used table partitioning by month (PostgreSQL's native PARTITION BY RANGE on created_at). This kept the active partition small (current month's logs) while historical data lived in separate partitions. Queries for the current month's audit logs scanned only the active partition (~1000 rows) instead of the entire audit history (~50,000 rows).

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

-- PostgreSQL table partitioning (HR Leave System)
CREATE TABLE leave_audit_logs (
  id BIGSERIAL,
  leave_id UUID NOT NULL,
  action VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  actor_id UUID NOT NULL,
  details JSONB,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);

-- Create monthly partitions
CREATE TABLE logs_2025_01 PARTITION OF leave_audit_logs
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-01-01') TO ('2025-02-01');
CREATE TABLE logs_2025_02 PARTITION OF leave_audit_logs
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-02-01') TO ('2025-03-01');
CREATE TABLE logs_2025_03 PARTITION OF leave_audit_logs
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-03-01') TO ('2025-04-01');

-- Auto-create future partitions (pg_partman or cron job)
-- Prisma raw query for audit logging
await prisma.$executeRaw`
  INSERT INTO leave_audit_logs (leave_id, action, actor_id, details)
  VALUES (${leaveId}, ${action}, ${actorId}, ${JSON.stringify(details)}::jsonb)
`;

-- Query only scans relevant partition
SELECT * FROM leave_audit_logs
WHERE created_at >= '2025-03-01' AND leave_id = '...';
-- EXPLAIN shows: Scan on logs_2025_03 (not parent table)

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: The audit log query for the current month was fast (20ms), but querying the last 6 months for a compliance report took 15 seconds — even though the total data was only 5000 rows.

Investigation: Manoj ran EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the 6-month query and saw it scanning all 6 partitions, but each partition scan was doing a sequential scan instead of using the index on leave_id.

Root Cause: The index was created on the parent table, but PostgreSQL table partitioning requires indexes to be created on each partition individually (pre-PG 11 behavior). The partitions inherited the parent table's columns but not its indexes.

Fix: In PostgreSQL 11+, creating an index on the parent automatically creates it on all existing and future partitions. Manoj recreated the index: CREATE INDEX ON leave_audit_logs (leave_id, created_at), which auto-propagated to all partitions. The 6-month query dropped from 15s to 150ms.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Forgetting to create partitions for future monthsUse pg_partman or a cron job to auto-create 3 months ahead
Indexes not inherited by partitions (old PG)Use PostgreSQL 11+; create index on parent table
Querying without partition key (scans all)Always include the partition key column in WHERE clause
Hot partition (all writes to current month)Acceptable for time-series; for non-time data, use hash partitioning

7. Validation Strategy

  • EXPLAIN test: Verify queries only scan relevant partitions
  • Partition creation test: Verify auto-creation script works for next month
  • Cross-partition test: Query spanning 6 months returns correct results
  • Cleanup test: Drop old partitions — verify no impact on current queries

Debugging Data Partitioning Issues

1. Definition & Context

Data partitioning bugs typically manifest as: missing data (insert goes to wrong partition or no partition matches), query scan all partitions (partition pruning not working), constraint violations (unique constraints across partitions), and partition management failures (missing future partitions cause insert errors).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, on January 1st, the audit logging started failing with: ERROR: no partition of relation "leave_audit_logs" found for row. The leave approval flow broke because the audit insert was part of the same transaction as the leave status update.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

-- Fix 1: Default partition (safety net)
CREATE TABLE logs_default PARTITION OF leave_audit_logs DEFAULT;

-- Fix 2: Auto-create partitions 3 months ahead
-- Run monthly via cron or pg_cron
DO $$
DECLARE
  start_date DATE := date_trunc('month', CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '1 month');
  end_date DATE;
  partition_name TEXT;
BEGIN
  FOR i IN 1..3 LOOP
    end_date := start_date + INTERVAL '1 month';
    partition_name := 'logs_' || to_char(start_date, 'YYYY_MM');
    
    IF NOT EXISTS (
      SELECT 1 FROM pg_class WHERE relname = partition_name
    ) THEN
      EXECUTE format(
        'CREATE TABLE %I PARTITION OF leave_audit_logs FOR VALUES FROM (%L) TO (%L)',
        partition_name, start_date, end_date
      );
      RAISE NOTICE 'Created partition: %', partition_name;
    END IF;
    
    start_date := end_date;
  END LOOP;
END $$;

-- Prisma migration to add partition creation to deployment
// prisma/migrations/add_partitions.sql
// Run as part of deployment pipeline

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: On January 1st 2026, all leave approvals failed with 500 errors. The error logs showed: no partition of relation "leave_audit_logs" found for row.

Investigation: Manoj checked the existing partitions: SELECT tablename FROM pg_tables WHERE tablename LIKE 'logs_%'. The latest partition was logs_2025_12. No partition existed for January 2026.

Root Cause: The partition creation was a manual process. When Manoj created the initial partitions in January 2025, he created 12 months ahead (through December 2025) but forgot to set up automatic partition creation for subsequent months.

Fix: Three changes: (1) Created the missing logs_2026_01 partition immediately to unblock production. (2) Added a DEFAULT partition as a safety net so unmatched rows never fail. (3) Set up a pg_cron job to automatically create partitions 3 months ahead, running on the 1st of each month. Added a monitoring alert that fires if no partition exists for the next 2 months.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Missing partition causes insert failureAlways have a DEFAULT partition + auto-creation cron
Default partition grows unboundedMonitor default partition size; alert if non-empty
Unique constraint across partitions doesn't workInclude partition key in unique constraint
Partition too fine-grained (daily for low volume)Choose granularity based on data volume: monthly for <10K/month

7. Validation Strategy

  • New year test: Set system clock ahead, verify inserts succeed for future months
  • Default partition test: Insert with far-future date, verify it goes to default partition
  • Cron test: Verify partition creation script is idempotent (safe to run multiple times)
  • Monitoring test: Delete next month's partition, verify alert fires within 1 hour
